name.es: Pan de centeno del Valais (Walliser Roggenbrot)

description.en: An almost pure rye hearth loaf from the Swiss canton of Valais, AOP-protected and built for the mountains: a long, cool sourdough rise, a thick cracked crust, and a dense, tangy crumb once meant to feed a village for months.
description.de: Ein fast reines Roggen-Hearth-Brot aus dem Schweizer Wallis, AOP-geschützt und für die Berge gemacht: langer, kühler Sauerteiggang, dicke, aufgerissene Kruste und dichte, würzige Krume – einst monatelang haltbares Dorfbrot.
description.es: Un pan de hogaza de centeno casi puro del Valais suizo, con denominación de origen AOP y pensado para la montaña: larga fermentación fría en masa madre, corteza gruesa y agrietada, y miga densa y ácida que antaño alimentaba a un pueblo entero durante meses.
description.fr: Un pain de campagne presque entièrement au seigle, originaire du Valais suisse, protégé par l'AOP et conçu pour la montagne : longue pousse au levain froid, croûte épaisse et craquelée, mie dense et acidulée qui nourrissait jadis un village pendant des mois.

origin.note: Holds AOP (protected designation of origin) status in Switzerland; historically the everyday bread of Valais's high alpine villages, often baked only a few times a year in communal ovens.

ingredients.note: coarse and fine rye wholemeal combined

keeping.note: Wrap in linen or paper; this alpine keeping bread stays good a week or more and slices thinner as it rests.

summary.en: The evening before, stir warm water, rye wholemeal and a spoonful of active rye starter with a little salt into a thick paste. Cover and let it ferment 12–16 hours at room temperature.
Add the remaining rye meal, warm water and salt to the ripe sourdough. Mix just until it comes together into a heavy, cohesive dough, there's no gluten network to develop, so don't overwork it.
Cover and let the dough rest about an hour to 90 minutes at room temperature, keeping it warm; rye sourdough performs best around 29°C.
Divide into two pieces and shape each by hand into a tapered, cone-like round. Roll the outside in rye meal and set on a floured cloth.
Let the loaves proof uncovered for an hour to 90 minutes, until deep cracks open across the floured surface, the traditional cue that they're ready to bake.
Bake in a very hot oven without steam, venting briefly after a few minutes and dropping the temperature partway through. Bake about 50 minutes until deeply crusted.
Let the loaves cool completely before cutting. This bread is dense and intense, traditionally sliced paper-thin for alpine cheese and dried meats.

source.adaptation: Optional soaked walnuts from the source omitted for the plain version; rounded to two 600 g loaves.
